    if n['color'] == 'G':

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The G objects (dark gray pixel) didn't change position. The boundary is still [[3, 1], [3, 30], [31, 30], [31, 1]]. The only change is the timer/HUD element (id=2) went from 2 to 3 pixels.
So clicking on the panels doesn't seem to change the right approach. Let me reconsider the game mechanic.
Looking at the image, the left panel has a yellow square inside it, and the right panel has a yellow vertical bar inside it. The gray area on the left has a dark gray pixel in the middle. The checkerboard on the right has two yellow objects.
Maybe the two yellow objects on the right side are the "targets" that I need to match. The gray bars are the "switches" that I need to toggle to select which yellow object to move.
Let me try clicking on the gray bars. In level 1, I removed all 6 gray bars. In level 2, maybe I need to remove specific gray bars to match the yellow objects.
Looking at the two yellow objects on the right side:
- Top yellow object (id=12) is at rows 7-11, cols 44-49.
- Bottom yellow object (id=45) is at rows 24-25, cols 45-48.
The gray bars are at rows 33-47, cols 7-55.
Let me think about the pattern of gray bars. The gray bars are arranged in a 4x8 grid. The left 4 columns are on the gray area and the right 4 columns are on the white area.
Maybe I need to remove the gray bars that correspond to the yellow objects' shape.
Let me try clicking on one of the gray bars on the right side (white area) first.

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on a gray bar on the right side (white area)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 44}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
